source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Justice Services Online (JSO): Family Online Filing Portal
text: Counsel and parties in family law and child protection cases are expected to file or request issuance of their documents electronically, and to pay any associated fees, through the family online filing portal on the Ministry's Justice Services Online (JSO) platform. Uploading a document to Case Center does NOT constitute filing the document. Unless the Court orders otherwise or a rule or provision of this Practice Direction provides otherwise, only documents that have already been filed with the Court may be uploaded to Case Center. Electronic filings must comply with restrictions on the length of material. Filed materials should also include any prior orders or endorsements that were issued and that are relevant to the request(s) being made.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Institutional Litigants and Counsel Must File Their Family Documents Online
text: Institutional litigants (such as a children's aid society, the Family Responsibility Office, Office of the Children's Lawyer and Ontario Works) and counsel shall use Justice Services Online to submit documents to the court for filing or issuance. Exceptions: (1) Documents for a court event or deadline that is 3 business days or less away (e.g. protection applications where a child has been brought to a place of safety); (2) Documents for an urgent hearing; (3) Documents in support of a sealing motion or subject to a sealing order; (4) Documents relating to writs of seizure and sale under Rule 28 of the Family Law Rules. Only documents falling under these exceptions may be filed or issued in person or by email by institutional litigants.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Limited Filing at the Court Counter
text: Self-represented parties are encouraged to use the portal but may file documents in person at the courthouse. A self-represented party who is unable to file documents through the Family Submissions Online portal or by email (for example, for accessibility-related reasons or technology limitations), can also contact the Accessibility Coordinator at the courthouse where the matter is being heard. Any paper document that is filed at the court counter will be returned to the party once it has been digitized by court staff. The party is required to retain the original and produce it on request (Family Law Rules 1.1(10) and (11.1)).

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Limited Filing by Email
text: The Court will accept limited filing by e-mail at the specific e-mail addresses indicated in each region's notice or practice direction for the following matters: (1) urgent matters, including requests for an urgent hearing; (2) documents that must be filed for a court event or deadline that is less than 3 business days away; (3) where a court order requires parties to file by email. The following documents should NOT be filed through the family online filing portal, but directly emailed to the court or filed in person at the court counter: (1) documents relating to writs of seizure and sale under Rule 28 of the Family Law Rules; and (2) documents in support of a sealing motion or subject to a sealing order.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Standard Document Naming Protocol
text: When documents are submitted to the court in electronic format, the document name must be saved as follows: Document type including the form number; Type of party submitting the document; Name of the party submitting the document (including initials if the name is not unique to the case); and Date on which the document was created or signed, in the format DD-MMM-YYYY. Examples: 'Financial Statement Form 13.1 – Respondent – A. Wong – 21-NOV-2021'; 'Case Conference Brief Form 17A – Applicant – G. Singh – 13-MAR-2022'. Document names shall not include firm-specific naming conventions, abbreviations, or file numbers.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Court Fees
text: Court fees are prescribed by regulation and are generally payable at the time a document with an associated fee is submitted to the court. Where a document is submitted through the family online filing portal, payment is made through the portal. Where a document is submitted in person, payment is made in person. Where a document is submitted by email or mail, payment can be made over the phone through a secure credit card transaction or by mail using a cheque and must be processed before the document will be accepted for filing/issuance. Parties who cannot afford to pay court fees may request a fee waiver certificate.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Uploading to Case Center
text: Case Center is an online platform where judges, counsel, parties, and court staff view electronic court documents before and during hearings. All properly filed court documents must be uploaded to Case Center regardless of whether the hearing is in person or virtual, with certain exceptions. This step is different from filing documents with the court. Documents uploaded to Case Center for use at a hearing should have already been filed. Key requirements: (1) Only documents that have been accepted and stamped should be uploaded; (2) Parties must upload their stamped court documents into the appropriate Case Center bundle promptly after receiving confirmation; (3) Trial Scheduling Endorsement Forms and draft orders should be provided in editable Word format; all other documents must be uploaded in PDF format; (4) Upload documents into the specific bundle created for the hearing, do NOT upload into the Master Bundle; (5) At least one day before the event, parties must upload a Participant Information Form.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Documents That Should Not be Uploaded to Case Center
text: Case Center is NOT currently approved for the following family cases or documents: (1) Child protection matters and other cases brought under the Child Youth and Family Services Act; (2) A document that is subject to a sealing order or for which a sealing order is sought; (3) Unredacted materials in support of a motion for removal as a lawyer. For documents subject to a sealing order, the Form 14: Notice of Motion for the sealing order can be uploaded to the motion bundle in Case Center after it is filed, but the unredacted document proposed to be sealed must be emailed to the Trial Coordination Office. For motions to remove a lawyer under subrule 4(12) and (13), parties shall only upload the notice of motion and affidavits of service to Case Center.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Financial Disclosure
text: Family litigants are expected to exchange full and frank financial disclosure as early as possible in the case to avoid unnecessary delay and expense. The Court expects that all reasonable efforts will be made to provide this disclosure to the other party in advance of the case conference. Subrule 13(3.1) of the Family Law Rules addresses the documents that must be included with a parties' financial statement where child or spousal support has been sought. If disclosure cannot be resolved prior to the case conference, the party seeking that disclosure must include in their materials a list of the outstanding disclosure in accordance with subrule 13(11.01). Costs may be awarded pursuant to subrules 17(18) or 24(7) where a party has failed to comply with their disclosure obligations. All disclosure issues should be addressed in advance of the settlement conference. Any necessary motions for disclosure must be heard in advance of the settlement conference.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Automatic Disclosure Orders, Rule 8.0.1
text: Rule 8.0.1 of the Family Law Rules directs the court to issue an automatic order when certain claims are made in an Application, Motion to Change, or Answer. This order requires parties to comply with their financial disclosure responsibilities, so the case conference is productive. The party who receives an automatic order from the court must serve the order on every other party in the case according to subrule 8.0.1(5). A party who has not made all reasonable efforts to comply with their disclosure obligations in advance of the case conference may be responsible for the other party's costs.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Font Size and Spacing for Conferences
text: All documents filed for a family conference or motion must be prepared using a font size of no less than 12 point and double spacing.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Restrictions on Filing Conference Materials (Page Limits and Attachments)
text: Case conference briefs shall not exceed 8 pages, plus permissible attachments and additional documents required by the Family Law Rules. This 8-page limit includes the brief itself (Form 17A) and any additional pages of facts and/or arguments attached as an appendix or schedule. Settlement conference briefs shall not exceed 12 pages, plus permissible attachments. This 12-page limit includes the brief itself (Form 17C) and any additional pages attached as an appendix or schedule. Trial management conference briefs (Form 17E) are no longer required; instead litigants must file the completed Trial Scheduling Endorsement Form. Permissible attachments (not included in page limits): (1) parenting assessments, OCL reports, and Voice of the Child Reports; (2) documents establishing a child's educational needs; (3) lists of outstanding disclosure; (4) income or business valuations, pension valuations, or real estate appraisals; (5) proof of income for the relevant periods; (6) domestic contracts relevant to the issues in dispute. Additional required documents (also not included in page limits): previous relevant orders/endorsements, updated Financial Statements, Net Family Property Statements, litigation expert reports, offers to settle, support calculations, terms of recognizance and police reports where applicable. Litigants must NOT include voluminous texts, emails and/or social media postings as attachments. Leave is required to file material beyond what is permitted, which will only be granted in exceptional circumstances.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Requirements to Confer in Advance of a Conference
text: Rules 17(3.1 and 3.3) of the Family Law Rules direct that before a conference, parties are to confer or, if unable to do so, make best efforts to confer with every other party about (i) each party's outstanding requests for financial disclosure, (ii) any other procedural matters, and (iii) a resolution of the outstanding issues. Parties are exempted from this requirement if they are prohibited from such communication by court order or terms of recognizance, or there are concerns about family violence and the alleged abusive party is not represented by a lawyer. Failure to confer prior to a conference could result in a postponement and an order for costs.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Confirmation Forms - Conferences
text: Each party to a conference must deliver to the court a fully completed Form 17F (Confirmation of Conference) no later than 2:00 p.m. three (3) business days before the conference. Each party must then immediately upload the confirmation forms to the related event bundle in Case Center. Form 17F Confirmations must only list the specific issues to be addressed at that conference and the specific materials the judge should review. Where Form 17F Confirmation forms have not been properly completed, given to the court and uploaded to Case Center by the appropriate deadline by at least one party, the conference will not be heard on the scheduled day without the Court's permission.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Combined Case/Settlement Conference
text: Parties can request the Court's permission to move directly to a combined case conference/settlement conference as the first step in the case. Rule 17(7.1) allows parties to make this request after they have tried to resolve the issues in dispute through mediation or a Legal Aid Ontario settlement conference, provided they can confirm that: the dispute resolution process included a screening for power imbalances and domestic violence; no motions for a temporary order are pending or contemplated; and financial disclosure has been provided. These requests can be made by filing a 14B Motion Form along with the Form 17G: Certificate of Dispute Resolution. Where both parties consent, they can also request this process if they have participated in another dispute resolution process (e.g. collaborative family law) and there are no outstanding temporary issues and financial disclosure has been provided.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Settlement Conferences and Trial Scheduling Conferences
text: The primary purpose of the settlement conference is to settle or at least narrow the issues in dispute. If the case is not settled at the settlement conference, one additional purpose is to identify witnesses and other evidence for trial, estimate time needed for trial, and where appropriate, schedule the case for trial. If the case has not settled at the conclusion of the settlement conference, the court shall determine if the Trial Scheduling Endorsement Form can be completed at the conference or shortly thereafter. A trial date will not be made available until the court has reviewed and endorsed the complete Trial Scheduling Endorsement Form. In exceptional circumstances, the court may provide litigants with a trial date before the court has endorsed the form, but the form must be finalized no later than 60 days in advance of the trial.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Trial Management Conferences
text: A trial management conference should be held in all family cases that have not been resolved at or before the settlement conference. Form 17E: Trial Management Conference Brief is no longer required. Instead, the following documents must be filed in advance of the TMC by the deadlines in rule 17(13.1): (1) The completed Trial Scheduling Endorsement Form must be filed and uploaded in Word format by either the Applicant or the party who requested the conference; (2) Each party must file an offer to settle all outstanding issues; and (3) Each party must file an outline of their opening statement for trial. Where the case has been settled and the trial is no longer required, one of the parties shall immediately advise the Trial Coordinator so that the trial date can be vacated.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Dispute Resolution Officer (DRO) Program
text: DRO programs are available in Kingston, Durham, Newmarket, Barrie, Toronto, Brampton, Milton, St. Catharines, Hamilton, Welland, Kitchener and London. DROs are senior family lawyers named by the regional senior judge to conduct select family case conferences. DROs shall hear all first case conferences for motions to change under Rule 15 and complete a Screening Report after each DRO Case Conference. DROs may hear first case conferences on other proceedings, attempt to identify/resolve/settle outstanding issues on consent, assist parties to organize their issues and disclosure, and assist parties in obtaining a signed consent order from a judge. DROs shall NOT write consents or draft orders on behalf of parties, make orders (on consent or otherwise), or award costs.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Dispute Resolution Conferences (DRCs) - Filing Requirements
text: Parties attending a DRC must comply with Rule 17 document requirements, including advance filing of: (1) a Form 17A: Case Conference Brief (on a motion to change, should at minimum include a copy of the previous order, documentation supporting the change in circumstance, and a description of the change being sought); (2) The deadline for serving and filing Form 17A prior to the DRC is six (6) business days for the applicant/moving party, and four (4) business days for the respondent; (3) A Form 17F: Confirmation Form must be filed not later than 2:00 pm, three (3) business days prior to the DRC; (4) Parties must upload their stamped court documents into the appropriate Case Center bundle promptly.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Short and Long Motions
text: The times for short and long motions for family proceedings can be found in your regional practice direction or notice to the profession. You may also contact your court location to find out whether your motion will be treated as a short motion or a long motion.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Font Size and Spacing for Motions
text: All documents filed for a family motion must be prepared using a font size of no less than 12 point and double spacing.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Restrictions on Filing Motion Materials (Page Limits and Attachments)
text: Each party is restricted to one primary affidavit in support of their position on the motion and cross-motion (if applicable) which shall not exceed: 12 pages of narrative for short or regular motions, and 20 pages of narrative for long motions. If a party also intends to rely on an affidavit that has been previously filed, the length of that affidavit is included in the page limit. This limit does not include third party and reply affidavits (max 5 pages each) or affidavits relating to a party's financial statement per Rule 13(12)(b). Exhibits to each party's affidavit shall be limited to only the necessary and relevant evidence and are generally expected not to exceed 10 pages. Litigants shall not include voluminous texts, emails, and/or social media postings. These page restrictions do NOT apply to motions for summary judgment or hearings with respect to the wrongful removal or retention of a child. Leave is required to file material beyond what is permitted, which will only be granted in exceptional circumstances.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Factums and Summaries of Argument
text: Factums or summaries of argument are required for all long family motions (and in the Toronto Region, they are also required on short motions) unless otherwise directed by a case conference judge. No factum or summary of argument may exceed 20 pages (double spaced and 12-point font) unless leave is granted. The times for service and filing of factums shall be in accordance with the times for service and filing of other motions materials under the Family Law Rules, unless a region-specific Practice Direction states otherwise. The factum should only include cases counsel or the party intends to refer to in oral argument. Each party's factum shall hyperlink authorities to a publicly available, free website such as CanLII. Each time a case is cited, it must include a paragraph reference to the case, with the applicable paragraph also hyperlinked.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Confirmation Forms - Motions
text: Each party to a motion must deliver to the court a fully completed Form 14C: Confirmation of Motion no later than 2:00 p.m. three (3) business days before the motions. Exceptions: Urgent motions brought without notice do not need to be confirmed; Long motions must be confirmed earlier than three (3) days in advance in several locations, as determined by the regional practice direction. Each party must then immediately upload the confirmation forms to the related event bundle in Case Center. Form 14C Confirmations must only list the specific issues to be addressed and the specific materials the judge should review. Where Form 14C Confirmation forms have not been properly completed, given to the court and uploaded to Case Center by the appropriate deadline by at least one party, the event will not be heard on the scheduled day without the court's permission.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Motions to Change Endorsement Forms
text: Subrule 15(24.1) directs the Court at the first attendance on a Motion to Change to determine the next steps in the motion, with a view to ensuring that the motion proceeds in the most efficient manner appropriate in the circumstances. Parties are encouraged to provide a draft Motion to Change Endorsement form with their materials for the first judicial conference to obtain early direction regarding the appropriate process for the case.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Restrictions on Adjournments
text: Some Regions have specific policies that require permission for adjournments, even on consent. It is the Court's expectation that matters will not need to be adjourned because counsel and/or litigants have not spoken ahead of time and/or taken the necessary steps for the matter to proceed as scheduled. Where a matter is adjourned because a party is not prepared, the Court may make a costs order against that party pursuant to subrules 17(18) or 24(7) of the Family Law Rules.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Materials Relied Upon Must Be Referenced During Hearing
text: Parties must bring to the attention of the court all relevant material facts and the authorities that they are relying on at the hearing. It is not sufficient to merely upload filed materials to Case Center. Materials that are not brought to the attention of the judicial officer at the hearing may not be considered.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Binding Judicial Dispute Resolution (Rule 43)
text: On January 22, 2025 the Family Law Rules were amended to add Rule 43: Binding Judicial Dispute Resolution in the Superior Court of Justice. Rule 43 allows parties to choose a Binding JDR as a summary process to resolve their family dispute, instead of a trial. Through Binding JDR, parties ask the same judge to try to assist them to settle their issues on consent and to make final orders about unresolved issues in a single hearing. The parties do not have an automatic right to call and cross examine witnesses at a Binding JDR hearing. Rule 43 currently applies in: the entire East, Central East, Central South, Northeast, Northwest and Toronto regions and Middlesex County (London). Note: Binding JDR is NOT yet available in the Central West Region (which includes Brampton).

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Orders
text: Judgments, endorsements, and orders of the Court are effective as of the date they are made, unless stated otherwise. Where a draft order is submitted online for issuing and entering, the registrar can electronically issue the order and email it to the requestor. Attending at the courthouse in person is not recommended unless it is time-sensitive, such as a family law restraining order, an order requiring police enforcement, or an issued order needed for an appeal.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Gowning for Counsel
text: Family counsel are NOT required to gown for the following court attendances: case conferences, settlement conferences, trial scheduling conferences, trial management conferences, and assignment court. Counsel must be gowned for all other in-person or virtual proceedings, regardless of whether the presiding judicial official is a judge or an associate judge.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: The Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) for Court Proceedings
text: All counsel, FLSPs, and litigants must guarantee accuracy when preparing materials for use in court proceedings, particularly when using AI. The misuse of AI is detrimental to the justice system. Most often, it occurs when counsel, FLSPs or litigants carelessly rely on fictitious authorities generated by AI (hallucinations). All legal information obtained using AI must be verified against trusted and authoritative sources. Potential sanctions include: public reprimand, ordering costs, fines and/or penalties, adjourning or dismissing a hearing/motion/conference/case, initiation of contempt proceedings, and referral to the Law Society of Ontario.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Manner of Address for Judges and Associate Judges
text: Judges and Associate Judges should be addressed in English as 'Your Honour' and in French as 'Votre Honneur'.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Mode of Proceeding - Family Matters
text: Presumptive modes of proceeding for family matters: (1) First appearances: heard virtually unless the Court specifies differently; (2) Early or urgent case conferences and triage courts: held by videoconference; (3) Urgent motions: heard by videoconference unless the Court specifies differently; (4) Case conferences, settlement conferences, and trial management conferences with a settlement focus: held IN PERSON unless a different method is approved in advance; (5) Trial scheduling conferences, other TMCs, and assignment court: heard by videoconference; (6) Motions for procedural relief and motions on consent: conducted in writing; more complex procedural motions by videoconference; (7) Substantive regular/short motions: generally by videoconference (outside Toronto and Windsor); (8) Long motions: held IN PERSON unless the Court has agreed to a virtual attendance in advance at the case conference; contempt motions always in person; (9) Trials: held IN PERSON unless all parties consent to virtual and the Court approves.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Mode of Proceeding - Dispute Resolution Conferences
text: All Dispute Resolution Conferences will continue to be held by videoconference.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Privacy - Public Access to Family Court Files
text: Under Section 137 of the Courts of Justice Act, the public is entitled to access any document filed in a civil court proceeding, including a family proceeding, unless an Act or court order provides otherwise. Rule 1.3 of the Family Law Rules requires a member of the public to give 10 days' written notice to the parties in a case before they may access a family court file involving decision-making responsibility, parenting time, or contact with respect to a child, orders under Part I of the Children's Law Reform Act (parentage), or international child abduction. After receiving notice, a party who wishes to restrict access has 10 days to file a 14B motion.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Mediation and Other Court-Connected and Community Resources
text: Litigants are encouraged to access parenting education programs, counselling services, supervised contact/parenting time, parenting coordination and other related services. Pursuant to Rule 8.1, parties are required to attend a Mandatory Information Program (MIP) at an early stage of the proceeding with limited exceptions. Affordable, court-connected mediation services are available at all Superior Courts. Family Court Support Workers provide direct support to victims of family violence who are involved in the family court process. Indigenous Family Court Workers assist separating couples who are Indigenous and involved in family court.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Legal Advice
text: Self-represented litigants are recommended to seek legal advice in advance of court attendances. Resources include: Legal Aid Ontario (1-800-668-8258 or www.legalaid.on.ca); Pro Bono Students Canada's Family Justice Centre; JusticeNet (www.justicenet.ca); Family Law Limited Scope Service project (www.familylawlssp.ca); Advice and Settlement Counsel Project in Toronto (www.ascfamily.com); and the Law Society of Ontario's Law Society Referral Service, which provides a free initial consultation of up to 30 minutes.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Ensuring the Integrity of Scheduled Trials, Hearings, and Appeals
text: Where a date for trial or other hearing has been set, the trial or hearing is expected to take place on that date. By agreeing to a trial or other hearing date, a lawyer or party is presumed to have made a commitment to appear on that date and to be bound not to make any other conflicting commitments. When setting a date for trials, appeals or other hearings, every lawyer or party has a duty to disclose previous commitments to another court that may conflict with a proposed date.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Communicating with Court, Staff and Trial Coordinators
text: When communicating by email with court filing and trial coordination offices, counsel and self-represented parties should include in the subject line: Level of Court (SCJ), Type of Matter (Family; Child Protection), File Number (indicate NEW if no court file number exists), Originating Court Location, Type of Document, Style of Cause, and Date of Event. Copy all parties on emails sent to the court. Counsel and parties shall not communicate directly with a judge unless the court directs otherwise.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Publication Bans
text: Any person seeking a discretionary order restricting publication of any Superior Court proceeding must serve and file a notice of motion or application and any supporting materials, in accordance with the applicable procedural rules. The requesting party must complete and submit the Notice of Request for Publication Ban form available on the Superior Court of Justice website. The notice period for submitting a Notice of Request for a Publication Ban is the same as the notice period under the Family Law Rules for serving and filing a Notice of Motion.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Early Judicial Intervention and Procedural Direction
text: Access to an early attendance before a judge can help parties (i) attempt to put temporary arrangements in place to avoid the need for a motion or other urgent attendance and (ii) obtain procedural directions. In many centres in Ontario, these issues can be canvassed at a case conference within a month and a half of the start of the case. Where caseload pressures do not allow early access to a case conference, several court locations provide an opportunity for litigants to seek the Court's assistance at an early, brief attendance (e.g. 15-20 minutes). Regional practice directions will provide further direction regarding availability, limited materials, limits on issues, and how these attendances should be scheduled.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Providing Accurate Estimates of Required Hearing Time
text: Parties must give careful consideration to what is to be covered in the hearing time, the pace at which documents and authorities can reasonably be reviewed, and the time needed for oral argument. Inaccurate estimates may result in a case being adjourned and rescheduled for a realistic time estimate with no expedition of the rescheduling. There may also be costs consequences.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Early Organization of Long Motions
text: Last minute adjournments of long motions must be avoided so that court time is not wasted. Parties are strongly encouraged to file materials for long motions in advance of the regular timelines under the Family Law Rules so that these motions can proceed as scheduled. Where possible, these timelines should be addressed at the case conference.

source: Consolidated Provincial Practice Direction for Family Proceedings
heading: Right of Appearance Pilot Project, Law Society of Ontario
text: Effective January 17, 2022, lawyer candidates (articling students, Law Practice Program students, Integrated Practice Curriculum students, and lawyer candidates who have not yet been called to the bar but have a Law Society-approved supervision agreement) may appear on certain events in a family law case without needing advance permission from the Court as required by Family Law Rule 4(1)(c). They must (i) be prepared with full instructions for all matters expected to be addressed and (ii) be appropriately supervised by a lawyer in their firm. A supervising lawyer with knowledge of the matter must be available on-call.
